questions.question: In the hierarchy of exposure controls, why does an exposure-room door interlock rank above a radiographer's careful attention?
options: Interlocks are less expensive to maintain than recurring safety training
Regulations give no credit to administrative controls or human actions
An engineered control works automatically regardless of human behavior, while a worker's attention degrades under fatigue and schedule pressure
Interlocks remove the need for post-exposure surveys
questions.explanation: The hierarchy of controls puts engineering and shielding at the top precisely because they do not depend on a person doing the right thing every time. Worker care sits at the bottom — it is the layer most likely to fail under fatigue — and no control, however strong, eliminates the survey routine.

questions.question: Why must the exposure device AND the guide tube be surveyed after every exposure, even when the crank retracted with completely normal feel?
options: To document the boundary dose rate for the utilization log
Because a stuck or disconnected source can leave the crank feel, turn count, and device appearance completely normal while the source sits unshielded in the guide tube
To verify the film received the planned exposure before it is processed
Because two consecutive meter readings must be averaged for the record
questions.explanation: If the pigtail parts from the drive cable, the cable cranks home normally and the device may even lock — while the source sits in the guide tube or exposure head. The post-exposure survey of the device and the full guide tube is the only step that catches this failure, which is behind some of the worst injuries in the trade.

questions.question: Your post-exposure survey shows a high field along the guide tube — the source did not retract. Your immediate first action is to:
options: Reach in with gloved hands to free the pigtail before more dose accumulates
Take one more short exposure to try to re-seat the source
Disassemble the guide tube at the device to inspect the connector
Back away to maximize distance, keep everyone out of the area, and initiate the emergency procedure, notifying the RSO
questions.explanation: A source stuck out of its shield is a radiological emergency. The response is built on the physics you already know — maximize distance first, minimize time, use shielding and remote tools — plus securing the area and notifying the RSO. Gloves stop nothing, and unauthorized hands-on recovery is never permitted.

questions.question: With the source locked in the fully shielded position, your meter reads 75 mR/h at one meter from a gamma exposure device. What does this reading tell you?
options: The device exceeds the 50 mR/h limit at one meter — it is out of compliance and comes out of service for qualified evaluation
The reading is acceptable because the limit at one meter is 200 mR/h
The reading is acceptable because anything under 100 mR/h is within tolerance at any distance
The reading is meaningless unless it is taken during an exposure
questions.explanation: A locked gamma exposure device may read no more than 200 mR/h at any exterior surface and no more than 50 mR/h at one meter. 75 mR/h at a meter signals a shielding fault or an incompletely retracted source — the 200 mR/h figure is the surface limit, not the one-meter limit.

questions.question: Who may repair a defective lock or drive mechanism on a radiographic exposure device?
options: The radiographer in the field, provided the manufacturer's manual is on the truck
Any Level II radiographer, after notifying the customer's safety department
Only qualified, authorized personnel under the licensee's program — the radiographer's job is to find the defect, tag the device, and remove it from service
The crew supervisor, provided the repair is documented in the daily log
questions.explanation: Exposure devices are never field-repaired. Maintenance of the lock, drive mechanism, and source path is performed only by qualified, authorized personnel, and worn parts like drive cables are replaced on the manufacturer's schedule — not when they finally fail on a job. The radiographer's duty is to detect, tag, remove from service, and record.

questions.question: A device label shows 100 curies of Ir-192 with an assay date 74 days ago. About how much activity is in the camera today, and why does the label carry that date?
options: 100 Ci — sealed sources do not lose activity while in service
About 50 Ci — one 74-day half-life has elapsed, and the assay date is what lets anyone decay-correct the labeled activity
About 25 Ci — two half-lives have elapsed since the assay
About 90 Ci — gamma sources decay only a few percent per quarter
questions.explanation: Iridium-192's half-life is 74 days, so the labeled 100 Ci is roughly 50 Ci today. 10 CFR 34.20 requires the user-applied label to state the activity and the date it was last measured precisely so a responder, inspector, or radiographer can decay-correct the number.

questions.question: At a temporary field job site (not a permanent radiographic installation), radiography may be performed only when:
options: A single certified radiographer is present and wearing an alarming ratemeter
The Radiation Safety Officer is physically present at the job site
The customer provides a designated fire watch at the boundary
At least two qualified individuals are present — the radiographer plus another radiographer or a trained radiographer's assistant
questions.explanation: 10 CFR 34.41 prohibits radiography at any location other than a permanent radiographic installation unless at least two qualified individuals are present. Dosimetry and surveillance requirements apply on top of — not instead of — the two-person rule, and the RSO is notified per procedure but need not be on site.

questions.question: What is the function of the exposure head required on the outermost end of the guide tube?
options: It stops the source assembly from passing out the end of the guide tube
It collimates the beam down to the film area to reduce scatter
It couples the drive cable to the source assembly's pigtail
It measures the dose rate at the exposure position during the shot
questions.explanation: 10 CFR 34.20 requires a guide tube whenever the source moves out of the device — projecting a source through open air is not permitted — and the exposure head on the tube's outermost end physically prevents the source assembly from traveling out the end. Collimation is separate ALARA hardware, not the exposure head's job.

questions.question: A collimator cuts the dose rate on its shielded sides by a factor of 100. By about what factor does that pull in the restricted-area boundary distance on those sides?
options: About ×100
About ×50
About ×16
About ×10
questions.explanation: Dose rate falls with the square of distance, so boundary distance scales with the square root of the shielding factor: √100 = 10. A bare 100 Ci Ir-192 source needs a 2 mR/h boundary near 158 m; collimated, the shielded sides pull in to about 16 m. The beam-port direction still demands full distance, and the boundary is always set by survey, never by the spec sheet.

questions.question: Which statement about source changers is correct?
options: Because the returning source has decayed, the changer may be stored unlocked while awaiting return shipment
A changer containing a sealed source is kept locked except under direct surveillance, and it receives formal inspection and maintenance at intervals not exceeding three months
Any certified radiographer may perform source exchanges whether or not the license authorizes them
A survey is required after an exchange only if the new source fails to seat on the first crank
questions.explanation: 10 CFR 34.23 keeps a changer containing a sealed source locked except under direct surveillance — a 'decayed' source is still tens of curies — and 10 CFR 34.31 puts changers on the same inspection program as cameras, at intervals not exceeding three months. Exchanges are performed only when the license authorizes them, and 10 CFR 34.49 requires surveys at every exchange.
